messages.content:
Awnser the following short as I was onlygiven 5 mins:

AP World History 

“In the twelfth year of his reign, to regulate foreign trade, Song Emperor Taizu [972 c.e.] decided to appoint a superintendent of maritime trade in the southern port city of Guangzhou, and afterward other superintendents in the ports of Hangzhou and Mingzhou as well. The emperor then appointed another superintendent to oversee the activities of all Arab, Sumatran, Javanese, Bornean, Philippine, and mainland Southeast Asian barbarians whose trade passed through China. These maritime merchants, both Chinese and barbarian, would usually take from China the following goods: gold, silver, strings of coins, lead, tin, many-colored silk, and porcelain. They would usually bring into China spices and aromatics, rhinoceros horn, ivory, coral, embers, pearls, fine steel, sea-turtle leather, tortoise shell, gemstones, foreign cloth, ebony wood, and other such things.”
History of the Song, official history of the Song dynasty, commissioned by the Mongol Chief Minister of Emperor Shundi of the Yuan (Mongol) dynasty, 1345

In your response, be sure to address all parts of the question. Use complete sentences; an outline or bulleted list alone is not acceptable.
 
Use the passage to answer all parts of the question that follows.
a) Identify ONE claim made in the passage about the policies of Chinese rulers.


b) Identify ONE way the passage illustrates the economic development of China under the Song dynasty.

a) One claim made in the passage about the policies of Chinese rulers is that they appointed superintendents to regulate foreign trade and oversee the activities of foreign merchants.

b) The passage illustrates the economic development of China under the Song dynasty by describing the trade of various goods between China and foreign merchants, indicating a thriving and active maritime trade network.

Awnser the following short as I was onlygiven 10 mins:

AP World History

“In the twelfth year of his reign, to regulate foreign trade, Song Emperor Taizu [972 c.e.] decided to appoint a superintendent of maritime trade in the southern port city of Guangzhou, and afterward other superintendents in the ports of Hangzhou and Mingzhou as well. The emperor then appointed another superintendent to oversee the activities of all Arab, Sumatran, Javanese, Bornean, Philippine, and mainland Southeast Asian barbarians whose trade passed through China. These maritime merchants, both Chinese and barbarian, would usually take from China the following goods: gold, silver, strings of coins, lead, tin, many-colored silk, and porcelain. They would usually bring into China spices and aromatics, rhinoceros horn, ivory, coral, embers, pearls, fine steel, sea-turtle leather, tortoise shell, gemstones, foreign cloth, ebony wood, and other such things.”
History of the Song, official history of the Song dynasty, commissioned by the Mongol Chief Minister of Emperor Shundi of the Yuan (Mongol) dynasty, 1345

In your response, be sure to address all parts of the question. Use complete sentences; an outline or bulleted list alone is not acceptable.

Use the passage to answer all parts of the question that follows.
a) Identify ONE claim made in the passage about the policies of Chinese rulers.


b) Identify ONE way the passage illustrates the economic development of China under the Song dynasty.

a) One claim made in the passage about the policies of Chinese rulers is that they appointed superintendents to oversee and regulate foreign trade.

b) The passage illustrates the economic development of China under the Song dynasty by highlighting the active maritime trade network and the exchange of various goods between China and foreign merchants. This suggests the growth and expansion of China's trade relationships and the increasing integration of the Chinese economy into the global market.
fix the following grammer: The unauthorized immigrant share of the population percentage per state of the united state of america
The unauthorized immigrant share of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America.
what's the graphic scale of the The unauthorized immigrant share of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America.

Apologies for the confusion, but it seems there is a misunderstanding regarding the nature of a "graphic scale." A graphic scale is a visual representation used in maps to indicate the relationship between distance on the map and the corresponding distance in the real world. It is not applicable in the context of representing the share of unauthorized immigrants as a percentage per state.

If you are looking for a visual representation of the unauthorized immigrant share of the population by state in the United States, a choropleth map or bar graph would be more suitable. These visualizations can effectively illustrate the variation in percentages across different states.
what’s the geographic scale of the The unauthorized immigrant share of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America.

The geographic scale of the unauthorized immigrant share of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America would be at the state level. It focuses on the variation in percentages of unauthorized immigrants within each individual state.
What is the scale of analysis of the The unauthorized immigrant share of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America

The scale of analysis for the unauthorized immigrant share of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America would be at the state level. It examines the variation in percentages of unauthorized immigrants within each state specifically.

What are some important differences you might see if you could change the “resolution” of the map?  That is, what might you see if you could show the data at a different scale of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America

If the resolution or scale of the map showing the unauthorized immigrant share of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America is changed, some important differences that might be observed include:

1. Regional Concentration: With a more granular resolution at a smaller scale, it would be possible to observe regional concentrations of unauthorized immigrants within individual states. This could provide insights into specific areas with larger or smaller percentages of unauthorized immigrants within a state.

2. Intra-State Variation: A higher resolution map would allow for a closer examination of variation within individual states. It could reveal areas within a state where the unauthorized immigrant population is concentrated or dispersed unevenly, highlighting local differences.

3. Border Proximity: A change in resolution may help highlight the relationship between proximity to international borders and the percentage of unauthorized immigrants. Areas closer to borders might exhibit higher percentages, while regions farther from borders could display lower percentages.

4. Urban vs. Rural Divide: A different scale could provide insights into the variations in unauthorized immigrant percentages between urban and rural areas within a state. It would be possible to identify urban centers with higher concentrations of unauthorized immigrants compared to rural or less populated areas.

5. Comparison with National Average: Altering the scale could facilitate a better comparison between states and the national average unauthorized immigrant percentage. It could reveal states that are significantly above or below the national average, indicating disparities in unauthorized immigrant populations across the country.

These differences in resolution would allow for a more nuanced understanding of the unauthorized immigrant population within states, highlighting regional, local, and demographic variations.
summerize

Changing the resolution or scale of the map representing the unauthorized immigrant share of the population as a percentage per state in the United States could reveal important differences. These differences might include regional concentrations, intra-state variation, proximity to borders, urban-rural divides, and comparisons with the national average. A higher resolution map would provide a more detailed understanding of unauthorized immigrant populations within states, highlighting specific areas and variations.
Does the choice of color/shading aid the readability of the map? Or, does it hinder it of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America

The choice of color and shading on a map representing the unauthorized immigrant share of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America can have a significant impact on its readability. 

If the colors and shading are carefully selected to create a clear and intuitive visual hierarchy, it can greatly aid readability. For example, using a gradual color gradient or a distinct color palette can help distinguish different percentage ranges and make the information easily interpretable. Additionally, using appropriate contrasting colors can further enhance readability.

However, if the color and shading are chosen poorly or without considering accessibility and visibility, it can hinder readability. For instance, using colors that are too similar or do not provide enough contrast may make it difficult to discern the different percentage levels, leading to confusion and misinterpretation of the data.

Therefore, it is crucial to choose colors and shading techniques thoughtfully and purposefully to ensure the map is readable and effectively communicates the unauthorized immigrant share of the population as a percentage per state.

What are some important differences you might see if you could change the “resolution” of the map? That is, what might you see if you could show the data at a different scale of Thematic Map  of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America

If the resolution or scale of the thematic map showing the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America is changed, several key differences might be observed:

1. Variation within States: A higher resolution map would enable a more detailed examination of the population percentages within individual states. It would allow for the identification of specific regions or counties with higher or lower population percentages compared to the overall state percentage. This could highlight significant demographic variations within states.

2. Urban vs. Rural Distribution: With a different scale, it would be possible to discern the distribution of the population percentages between urban and rural areas. This could reveal patterns of higher population percentages in urban centers and lower percentages in rural or less densely populated areas.

3. State Comparisons: Altering the scale of the map could facilitate a comparative analysis of population percentages between states. It would highlight states with higher or lower percentages, providing insights into regional differences in population distribution across the country.

4. Border Proximity: A change in resolution might help draw attention to the population percentages in regions close to international borders. It could reveal areas with higher percentages due to factors like immigration patterns or economic activities near the border.

5. Subtle Patterns and Trends: A higher resolution map could expose nuanced patterns and trends within states or regions that might not be apparent at a broader scale. This might include localized clusters of high or low population percentages, indicating specific demographic characteristics or influences.

By adjusting the resolution or scale, a more refined analysis of the population as a percentage per state of the United States would provide a deeper understanding of demographic variations and spatial patterns within and across states.

These differences include variations within states, distribution between urban and rural areas, comparisons between states, border proximity effects, and identification of subtle patterns and trends. Adjusting the scale allows for a more detailed analysis, revealing insights into demographic variations and spatial patterns at a finer level.
Does the choice of color/shading aid the readability of the map? Or, does it hinder it of Thematic Map of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America

The choice of color and shading on a thematic map representing the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America can have a significant impact on its readability.

If the colors and shading are selected carefully to create a clear visual hierarchy, it can aid readability. Using a thoughtful color palette and distinct shades can help effectively differentiate between different percentage ranges and make the map easier to interpret. Additionally, using contrasting colors can enhance readability by making the information visually clear.

However, if the color and shading choices are not well-thought-out or lack contrast, it can hinder readability. It may make it challenging to discern different population percentage levels and result in confusion or misinterpretation of the data.

Therefore, it is crucial to choose colors and shading techniques thoughtfully, ensuring they work together to create a visually clear and readable thematic map representing the population as a percentage per state of the United States.

How have the breakpoints been determined? Natural or equal-interval? Explain how you are able to know of Thematic Map of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America

Without specific information provided about the thematic map of the population as a percentage per state of the United States of America, it is impossible to determine how the breakpoints have been determined. The determination of breakpoints can vary depending on the purpose of the map, the data being represented, and the methodology used by the map creator.

However, in general, breakpoints on a thematic map can be determined using different methods, such as natural breaks (Jenks optimization) or equal intervals. 

1. Natural breaks (Jenks optimization): This method seeks to group data into distinct classes by minimizing within-class variance and maximizing between-class variance. It aims to find natural "breakpoints" or natural-groupings within the data based on clustering characteristics. This method can be effective in representing data with inherent clustering patterns.

2. Equal intervals: In this method, the data range is divided into equal intervals with an equal number of observations in each interval. This approach provides a consistent division of the data range but may not account for natural variation or clustering within the data.

To determine how breakpoints were specifically determined on a thematic map of population percentages per state, it is necessary to refer to the methodology provided by the map creator or consult the source of the map. The methodology section or documentation of the map would provide insights into how breakpoints were determined and the underlying rationale behind the chosen approach.

Breakpoints can be determined using methods such as natural breaks or equal intervals. The specific approach used in this case would need to be referenced from the map creator's methodology or documentation.
